content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for “there’s nothing else like this” movies.

**Score:** 78
**Body:** Looking for movies that are one of a kind. 

I watched House (1977) for the first time and I don’t think there is anything else quite like it. It’s just in a league of its own. 

Looking for suggestions that are just unique standouts.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Check out Mad God on Shudder. It’s a stop-motion horror/scifi/war movie that was worked on over a period of decades by one of the main guys who worked on the first Jurassic Park animatronics as a side project. It’s entirely his vision, and the imagery is weird, scary, and in a lot of places gross. The whole movie looks like it smells but I’ve never seen anything like it anywhere ever. 
2. Eraserhead 
3. The Gods must be crazy
4. Tampopo (1985)
5. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind hits that theme a bit, I think. It really captivated me.
6. The Holy Mountain
7. A Scanner Darkly 
8. The City of Lost Children (1995)
9. Swiss Army Man
10. Amelie

## 2. Can you recommend movies where the first half feels completely different from the second half like a total 180 shift in tone or story?

**Score:** 72
**Body:** I am talking  talking full 180 shifts that completely reframe the story, where tone, genre, or stakes suddenly transform in shocking, unforgettable ways that leave you rethinking everything you just watched.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. From Dusk Till Dawn (1996)
  - came here expecting it to be the only answer...slightly wrong
2. Full Metal Jacket
  - Also the comedy Stripes for the same reasons,  seemed like all the good stuff was when they were in basic training.
3. Sorry to Bother You
4. Parasite (2019)
5. The Place Beyond the Pines (2012)
6. The Cabin in the Woods
7. Barbarian 
8. Click. 

Second part slapped the funny out of me. 

## 3. Movies that take place over the course of a day?

**Score:** 61
**Body:** Hi there! I'm new here, and I'm looking for movie recommendations that take place over the course of a day (any genre, though I'm partial to thrillers).

Examples include:

\- Phone Booth

\- The Belko Experiment

\- Clue

\- Die Hard

\- Training Day

\- Would You Rather

\- The Descent

\- Game Night

\- Fly Paper

I find this subgenre to be so fun & exciting, so I'd love to watch more! Thank you in advance for your recommendations!

EDIT: Holy smokes! Thank you guys for all of your awesome suggestions! I am going down rabbit holes looking all of these up. Thanks guys!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 12 angry men.  A whole film in one jury room over one afternoon, watching personalities and ethics collide.

Also, before sunrise, and befoe sunset.  Do the right thing.

These are available on Criterion Collection Blu-ray, and they look terrific. 
2. Dazed and Confused 
3. Clerks
4. Go

Magnolia
5. Dog day afternoon. 
6. Groundhog Day

Run Lola Run 

Kids? I think?

Adventures in Babysitting
7. After Hours
8. Joyride starring Paul Walker - a relentless trucker goes all psychotic behind 3 friends on a road trip

Ferris Bueller's Day off - I think this is one the best of this single day genre. Absolutely fantastic comedy

Ready or not :  a commendable survival horror with a twist

The Cabin in the Woods : Under-rated horror i think


9. Empire Records 1995
10. Breakfast Club 

## 4. films where one person holds romantic feelings for another for atleast a decade

**Score:** 35
**Body:** sorry this sounds like such a strange request, but i realized a common thing in all the movies i enjoy watching is when one person in the relationship either waits a really long time for the other or has unrequited feelings for them for atleast a decade.

some examples off the top of my head

\- paro in the movie devdas, she waits 10 years for devdas  
\- in the mood for love  
\- the notebook  
\- the children’s hour

main trope doesn’t need to be romance, i just enjoy characters who experience this no matter how minimal it’s shown in the film

please im desperate !!!!

edit: good example of this is penelope of ithaca !! and am okay with non-american films !! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Forrest Gump
2. Past Lives.
It’s not for everyone, I really liked it.
3. Does it count if they don’t realise they have feelings? 

If so, then When Harry Met Sally is a good one. 
4. The Curious Case of Benjamin Button
5. Somewhere in Time
6. Remains of the day.
7. People We Meet On Vacation

In a way, When Harry Met Sally

Sweet Home Alabama
8. Past Lives
9. mamma mia! 

## 5. A Face In The Crowd (1957)

**Score:** 34
**Body:** I have to say this movie is in the same league as 'Citizen Kane' or 'Dr. Strangelove' - and it's *so* overlooked. It's a biting political commentary, a love story, and a psychological roller coaster. Another film it reminds me of is 'Whatever Happened to Baby Jane?' - it's that crazy. 

The list of names is Altman-esque - Andy Griffith [incredible in his first role], Patricia Neal [The Day the Earth Stood Still], Lee Remick [The Omen], Walter Matthau [Fail Safe], with cameos by Bennett Cerf, Burl Ives, Sidney Pollack, Mike Wallace, Walter Winchell, and directed by the great Elia Kazan.

The sets, lighting and camera work are creative, innovative and fully exploit the artistic potential of black and white.

Griffith gives an acting master class as a cynical drifter who becomes unimaginably powerful. It illustrates how a shameless demagogue can influence and deceive an entire country, with the assistance of the greediest and most ruthless of the oligarchs, who have learned to manipulate the media. 

The finale has the longest sustained tension-crescendo of any movie I can remember. I just re-watched it after a few years to be sure it was really as good as I remembered, and it was. Five Stars.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. We’re living that movie now. way ahead of its time. A great movie.
2. let's not forget that it was written by Budd Schulberg, who was a writer for On The Waterfront which was also directed by Kazan.
3. I got to see this in a theater with an introduction by Bill Corbett (Mystery Science Theater 3000/Rifftrax) about why it was his favorite movie. In addition to the cinematography being beautiful on a big screen, the sound design is excellent (the laugh track machine especially). It's definitely underappreciated.
4. Incredible film and by far Andy Griffith's best acting role. Given his later more wholesome image from his most popular roles Face also works as a sort of retroactive casting against type.
5. Love this movie. Very prescient to our time right now.
6. It was filmed down in the little town, where my mom was from in Arkansas. I had some relatives appear in it.  
  - Wow that's amazing
7. Not quite either of those other two films and I think it really lets the people in his audience off the hook. The more things change, the more they stay the same…at least in Mayberry.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What's your favorite Bryce Dallas Howard role?

**Score:** 2942

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|htinLom37opJDyuajR)  
She absolutely nailed this role and was quite likable as the main in the Village.  Most of her other roles are similar to Nosedive and you aren’t really supposed to root for her which is good because I don’t find her to be audience favorite.
  - https://preview.redd.it/nm9sdly2f8ah1.jpeg?width=720&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=fdd5e1a459a93f0b11a9225749c7aa9d62319faa

I've used this as a pfp/banner on and off.
  - The Village really is her most underrated performance. I feel like people sleep on that movie in general because of the twist, but she carries the whole thing.
  - To clarify the gif is from Black Mirror

2. I never realised that was her in Twilight.
3. Her episode of Black Mirror was pretty great. 
  - She nailed that fake persona.  Especially that laugh.  It was so satisfying seeing her finally lose her shit at the wedding.  I was so happy for her despite how messed up the situation was lol.  Naomi definitely fucked Greg.
4. I was going to say that I like Molly’s Game, but only now am learning that Bryce Dallas Howard and Jessica Chastain are two different people
  - I've heard this described as "same person, different font" and I know doppelganger is already a word, but it just delights me 
5. She was good in the social media episode of Black Mirror. 

## 2. Margot Robbie's casting as Harley Quinn remains one of the best casting decisions in comic book films.

**Score:** 550
**Body:** What are your thoughts on her portrayal of Harley Quinn?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I agree, she kills it.

I thought her standalone movie Birds of Prey was a lot better than its general reception too, just fun crazy colorful shit. What’s so bad about that, especially given how so many superhero movies are basically monochrome. Some of them need to get some color back in those cheeks.
  - Man I loved Birds of Prey, it was a such a fun riot, like Charlie’s Angels and Deadpool had a baby. Fuck all those chuds who review bombed it without even seeing it.
  - Birds of Prey is underrated as fuck. Ewan McGregor's performance as the villain was great & there's one particular scene where I felt disgusted by him >!(The one where he forces a girl at a nightclub to dance & strip because he thinks she's laughing at him)!<
    - Truly. He gives the perfect performance of a narcissist. Someone who treats everything he sees as relating to him. 
2. ![gif](giphy|xUA7aVW7WzU39xbJZK)
3. She has such a cute, crazy look! I love her❤
  - For real, she totally nailed that unhinged vibe without making it feel forced. The way she switches between playful and unhinged is just chef's kiss.
4. I'll throw in that Kaley Cuoco was also well cast in the animated series. 
5. ![gif](giphy|cmfPgjwPxPDK2ae6OQ)
6. Perfect casting.

![gif](giphy|CdxBRh8v9pVJIrd4J3)

## 3. Jacob Elordi or Callum Turner Should Not Be James Bond, Says Ex-007 Casting Director: ‘We Know So Much About Them’ and the Actor Should Be ‘Out of the Blue’

**Score:** 432

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jacob Elordi I think is a terrible choice. Callum Turner I could see doing well but I agree it should be a lesser known actor. Elordi though is truly awful for this role 
  - I don't feel like either of them are charismatic enough for the role, and that's such a huge part of bond. I'd love to see an unknown actor in the role if that means he's a better fit.
  - I hate Elordi at this point I feel like the guy is getting shoved in my face. He was FINE in Frankenstein but so overhyped. He's just... tall. That's it.
2. I'm pretty sure the only thing most people know about  Callum Turner is who he's married to 
  - Everyone knows that. 

Mrs. Callum Turner. 
3. Did they model the character from the new game on a real person? If so, that guy. In fact just soft launch new bond with that game as cannon. 
4. Ana Taylor Joy. Bond will be a lesbian who speaks Spanish which would be kinda cool, that and her eyes are all far apart 
  - https://preview.redd.it/7szait3l5bah1.jpeg?width=2700&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=c7dd4a6ed6fb58606a02c6f23d0053dfd87d5f5a
5. These dummies had Cavill just sitting there for years. Ridiculous. 
  - That was the guy who deserved to be Bond.

## 4. What’s your favorite phone call scene?

**Score:** 410
**Body:** What is that one phone call scene that’s so unforgettable for you?

For me it’s the phone call scene in The Departed (2006). The tension here is so palpable. The fact that Damon and DiCaprio say nothing here is so brilliant!

What’s your favorite phone call scene?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Terminator 2. Both impersonating someone else to try and trick the other. And of course Arnie comes out on top, with the clever wrong dog name bit.

2. https://preview.redd.it/0e8qwfuftaah1.jpeg?width=348&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=457bb4ead8b61b426cd5552f5bc1484751794325
3. ![gif](giphy|pQLmrZN8r1p0Q)

Heat

"Cause there is a dead man on the other end of this fuckin' line."
4. ![gif](giphy|l2YWy3OWspHaSatXO)  
***What’s your favourite scary movie?*** (Scream)
5. ![gif](giphy|PkqXwgP66glZ6)
6. Chris Evans' character in Scott Pilgrim vs the World.

"The next click you hear is me hangin' up.  The one after that is me pullin' the trigger."
7. Phone Booth - Colin Farrell 

https://i.redd.it/l8u4qvfpraah1.gif
  - This movie is incredible and highly underrated.

Farrell is phenomenal here.

"Oh."

1. JK Simmons as J. Jonah Jameson

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Movies where the director or actor are not the most notable contributor

**Score:** 80
**Body:** Typically when a name is attached to a movie it is either the director or an actor. You'd say "it a Spielberg film" or a "Adam Sandler film" and so on. However I've noticed there are rare cases where it's a non director or actor. I've thought of a few examples and was wondering if people could think of any others?

  
The Nightmare Before Christmas - Tim Burton (story)

Being John Malkovich, Adaptation, Eternal Sunshine..etc - Charlie Kaufman (writer)

  
Empire Strikes Back, Return of the Jedi - George Lucas (story,producer)

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Home Alone being considered a John Hughes movie

Joss Whedon's name was heavily marketed for Cabin In The Woods

Another example that comes to mind are films based on Stephen King books. They're always widely referred to as "Stephen King Movies" 
2. Some MGM musicals but specifically the Hollywood Revue of 1929 where it's a series of MGM contract actors and performers putting on their routines. The studio machine and producers making the big decisions take over the contributions from the directors and actors. 


Tron Legacy with Daft Punk's soundtrack.


Ray Harryhausen with his special effects. 


Busby Berkeley with his dance numbers when he's not credited as a director.


Walt Disney with the feature films he produced.






  - > Walt Disney with the feature films he produced

I could not in a million years tell you the directors or voice actors of Snow White, Pinnochio, Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Lady and the Tramp, Peter Pan, or other classic Disney movies.
3. A24 as a company had a little bit of this early days where the pull was a quirky trailer and the brand of A24. In horror, the hook or story can often be the notable draw over the less well know director or star. I can’t really think of many examples of heads of other departments being the main draw. 
  - I mean, this was basically Spielberg/Amblin in the 80s/90s - "Steven Spielberg Presents" lead many to believe [Gremlins](https://www.allaboutmovies.com.au/media/k2/items/cache/7fa3450298fb21ef3656f8fe9b6a07e4_XL.jpg), [Back to the Future](https://www.originalfilmart.com/cdn/shop/products/back_to_the_future_1985_advance_linen_original_film_art_a_600x.jpg), [The Goonies](https://m.media-amazon.com/images/I/71gdMZ9LHTL._AC_UF894,1000_QL80_.jpg), etc. were films directed by Spielberg.
4. Walt Disney. In the earlier days of Hollywood producers such as David O Selznick were the "badge" so to speak. Hitchcock although the director of Rebecca saw it as a Selznick film rather than his own.
5. The movie literally gets called [Tim Burton's The Nightmare Before Christmas](https://encrypted-tbn2.gstatic.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcSOgiKvYkIrCXt6eUC8cDtsMci6OPlooJ3BGr5XOOMP62zhrBgd) and so I didn't even know he wasn't the director until recently.
  - You could say Danny Elfman had as much creative input in that film as Burton. 
6. For musical films, the composer/performer is often more noteworthy.  Mamma Mia is an ABBA jukebox musical, and only incidentally part of Streep’s body of work.

Often the subject of a documentary is more important than the director.

Ray Harryhousen and Busby Berkeley are almost always more important contributors than the director assigned to their film.

Sometimes the stunt director is the most interesting contributor to a minor Hong Kong flick

Even putting aside superhero franchises, how the film fits into a body of work.  It is a Godzilla movie, or even  a Showa Era Godzilla movie.  

What is most noteworthy can change over time, in the beginning, say, Ian Fleming was far more noteworthy than a minor Scottish actor cast as Bond, later that changes to the actor, still later it changes to the producers.
7. Pretty much every movie Judd Apatow produced is credited to him even though he’s directed only a few of them.

Diablo Cody-penned movies like Jennifer’s Body, Juno, and Lisa Frankenstein are generally credited to her I think

## 2. One of my favourite things in uni was the courses that dissected single books. Does such a thing exist for films?

**Score:** 31
**Body:** Probably a dumb question. I didn't get more serious about my love of film until after university, so most of my experience will be speaking to taking a few lit courses at uni.

I also loved the courses that were close readings and thorough examinations of single, or a select few, books. I took courses on Joyce, Shakespeare, Russian Lit, etc. where ample time could be spent thoroughly dissecting the works. I'm wondering if anything exists like this for films and directors? Especially that is accessible to those of us who have graduated? I've seen a lot of YouTube videos that go over more popular films that are generally not what I'm interested in. I would, though, love if there were more academic readings of film, from Bresson to Melville, to Seijun Suzuki to current Slow Cinema/Transcendental Cinema filmmakers.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Roger Ebert used to do this, run a seminar where they just watched Citizen Kane for example. Since he is gone, we can’t have that, but we will have his commentary on the DVD forever.

Honestly, I think commentaries might be your best bet rather than YouTube, they tend to be done by very qualified film theorists and historians, as well as by the directors themselves sometimes. Ebert on Floating Weeds, for example, James Quandt on Pickpocket (since you like Bresson), Steven Soderbergh & Mike Nichols commentary for “The Graduate” are all fantastic!
  - I went to the last one of those he did. We spent a week going through No Country for Old Men shot by shot, it was amazing. He couldn’t contribute much because he was speaking through a robot at this point and said it was pretty frustrating. They still do them, they just have other critics and sometimes the filmmaker leading it. The year after no country, Herzog was there and we did Aguirre the Wrath of God. Good stuff. 


2. A friend would talk about his favorite undergraduate course being one where they watched In the Mood for Love *every week* for a full semester, each time discussing it under a new lens/interpretive schema. It's not quite the same as spending the whole course reading one very long & dense novel though. It's hard to imagine many films long enough & dense enough to work on that level. Godard's "Histoires" is one that maybe could - but even that is only like 5 hours long.
3. Not sure about a semester long academic offering, but a number of publishers have offered books that focus on a single film. BFI in particular but also Indiana University Press, while Wallflower Press have done a series on cult films they call Cultographies. These are often illuminating. The BFI Film Classics series is consistently good. 
4. Oh yes. I was very fortunate to take classes from--and later, as a master’s student--to serve as a teaching assistant for the great film scholar Amos Vogel, author of *Film as a Subversive Art.*

He did indeed teach a course in which we spent the entire semester studying a single film. In our case, it was Bernardo Bertolucci's *Before the Revolution*. By the end of the semester, I think I had the film memorized shot by shot--or at least I used to.

There are some outstanding books that devote themselves entirely to the history, production, interpretation, and cultural significance of a single film. The British Film Institute's Film Classics series is devoted to that. 

Augé, Marc. *Casablanca: Movies and Memory.* Minneapolis: University of Minnesota Press, 2004.

Bordwell, David. *Seven Samurai.* London: BFI Publishing, 1994.

Swaab, Peter. *Bringing Up Baby*. London: British Film Institute, 2011.

[BFI FILM CLASSICS SERIES](https://www.bloomsbury.com/us/series/bfi-film-classics/?__cf_chl_f_tk=9wyu2jbjq4k3LjXmWDpClpaRdiAi3aOch.2nqUc6oNQ-1782777012-1.0.1.1-pEc_IcpGBN_BuaMLPsWABOtU5AlVdwZ.XQD8FrKx1xI)
5. I had a friend who got to take a class entirely on watching and rewatching Jaws. 

They watched it about 16 times in a semester (once a week), analyzing it for first time viewing/impressions, then every week in other faacets including emotional impressions, plot analysis, camera movement and language, acting, script and story structure, sound and music, lighting, themes and philosophical arguments, etc. etc. etc. . They got to do essays on their impressions of it, how rewatching might have made them change how they think about films, etc.

I am so jealous that it was not offered by the time I made it to that school.
6. While not exactly what you're looking forward, there's a blindingly excellent 10-episode series on the history of film called The Story of Film: An Oddyssey that came out in 2011. It delves into all the subjects and directors you're talking about, spotlighting each of them. It goes all around the world, not just Hollywood. I'm currently watching in on Kanopy, our library streaming service. Not sure where else to find it. But I just wanted to throw that in there. I think you'd love it.
7. Like other comments, this ain’t a perfect fit, but I really enjoyed ZONA by Geoff Dyer, a book about Tarkovsky’s Stalker.  

I’d also recommend Weird Studies, a somewhat heady (yet not stuffy) podcast about art & philosophy. They have a few episodes on movies that I have enjoyed. Their most recent episode is on Alien, and it’s a good one.
8. well if u're ok with reading alongside watching i found film monograpghs get surprisingly close to that seminar feeling. a good one can spend an entire book unpacking a single film or director without feeling like surface level criticism...
9. A friend took a class at Berkeley on the films of the Coen brothers.

Unfortunately, I don't believe there's any material available online. I was, however, able to track down [a similar course](https://wheelercolumn.berkeley.edu/2021/09/09/modernity-and-the-films-of-the-coen-brothers/) being offered there through the English department (so probably not likely to cover things from as filmic of a perspective so much as a textual one), though in the wrong time period. It looks like this one covered *Blood Simple*, *Fargo*, and *A Serious Man*.

## 3. Labyrinths, not Mazes: a cross-analysis of Tarkovsky's "Stalker" & Oshii's "Angel's Egg" under the framework of Alain Robbe-Grillet's idea of the "New Novel"

**Score:** 15
**Body:** [ Is Art Like a Maze, or a Labyrinth? Tarkovsky's "Stalker," Oshii's "Angel's Egg," & The New Novel](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=H7GiA1LZg_4&t=3s)

Hi, thought some people on here might appreciate this video of mine - 

I cover Alain Robbe-Grillet (*Last Year at Marienbad; L'Immortelle*) and his theory of the "New Novel," which, in reductive terms, called for a radical reinvention of the novel/film/fiction at large.

Instead of creating art that has readily interpretable symbols (e.g., Eisenstein), the "New Novel" movement seeks to move past the idea that there is a deeper meaning to be deciphered by the viewer, and instead, wants us to revel in the raw beauty of images and the artwork itself (very similar to what Tarkovsky says: images, not symbols).

I take some of the ideas in his literary theory and essays on fiction and apply them to two very enigmatic films that people have long tried to "interpret" and "decipher," being *Stalker* (Andrei Tarkovsky, 1979) and *Angel's Egg* (Mamoru Oshii, 1985).

All of this is framed under the metaphor of the Maze vs. the Labyrinth. Not only are they vastly distinct structures, but in the context of experimental and seemingly unintelligible artwork, can function as cool metaphors for how to approach the search for meaning in artwork.

Cheers!

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Great video and analysis. It touches on an aspect of modern media consumption that I find really destructive to art - the prevalence of “Ending Explained” type content. I think a part of this phenomenon is that younger people simply haven’t developed their own analytical voice yet, but I think another thing that more insidiously contributes is the landscape of social media, lending an unearned authority to influencers. So when a casual media consumer engages with media they don’t intuitively understand, they pass off the analysis/interpretation to the influencer, and the majority adopt this homogenized viewpoint which flattens discourse. To me, it’s a very dissociated way to look at art, essentially removing the “communication” aspect which makes it so wonderful to me.

To put it in the lens of your Maze vs. Labyrinth metaphor, it’s a map-maker for a maze versus the user of said map. The map-maker needs a thorough and intimate understanding of the maze, such that every twist and turn becomes second nature to them, and the maze becomes more like a labyrinth, as every confounding twist coalesces into the grander experience of the maze. Compared to the viewer of the map, who effortlessly gets through the maze in one go, experiencing none of the same friction which led the map-maker to stop and analyze and understand. To them, the maze might also become more like a labyrinth, not through wholistic understanding but rather passivity.
  - Tonight I was watching the Noir The Big Sleep (1946) - I had seen it many years ago, but had forgotten most of it - and I found myself completely lost in the plot, just twist and revelation and clue and twist one after another...to the point I just relented and enjoyed every situation and stopped tracing every character name. It made me think about something. These style films were probably made for rewatch. You've gone through the labyrinth once, you go again. You know your way around a little better. And then again. I suspect people in the 1940s, 50s went to see the same movie more than one time. Sometimes this led to plot complexity, but I think more it also led to films being so well constructed - even if not reaching for masterpiece or "art" - that scenes or story arcs could be enjoyed multiple times. Now it seems sometimes that if you've read a synopsis and a few arguments online you kind of already feel like you've SEEN the film. Like, I don't really need to see Obsession (to take a random example), I feel I already have in a way.

Just some reflection on your thoughts on online commentary and the labyrinth.
2. I'm at work but I can't wait to watch. I love both of these movies, and my approach to art has been informed by raw experience over meaning ever since high school (when I read *Against Interpretation* for an English class). 

Thank you for sharing! 

## 4. Casual Discussion Thread (June 29, 2026)

**Score:** 2
**Body:** General Discussion threads threads are meant for more casual chat; a  place to break most of the frontpage rules. Feel free to ask for  recommendations, lists, homework help; plug your site or video essay;  discuss tv here, or any such thing.

**There is no 180-character minimum for top-level comments in this thread.**

**Follow us on:**

* [Letterboxd](http://letterboxd.com/truefilmreddit/)
* [Twitter](https://twitter.com/truefilmreddit)

The sidebar has a wealth of information, including the subreddit  rules, our killer wiki, all of our projects... If you're on a mobile  app, click the "(i)" button on our frontpage.

Sincerely,

David

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. Dual three act and mirror screen play

**Score:** 2
**Body:** The dual three acts are staked and mirrored.

For the first three acts the antagonist is powerful and the protagonist struggles to get a win. At the climax of the first act, the protagonist wins and becomes powerful. 

For the second three acts the protagonist is still powerful and the antagonist is trying to change the status quo, effectively becoming the mirror of the protagonist of the first three acts. 

The climax of the second three act is the protagonist prevails and the antagonist get defeated.

Any movies with this screenplay structure? 

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


---
